skill.name: deployment-validation-config-validate
skill.description: Validates deployment configuration files with schemas, environment rules, tests, runtime checks, migration guidance, and secure configuration handling.
skill.summary: Validate deployment configuration before release with schema, security, and environment checks.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but the external command and weak cryptography alerts are false positives caused by fenced Markdown examples and identifier matches. The skill is not malicious, but it advises reading configuration files including .env-style files and watching configuration paths, so users should avoid exposing real secrets during use.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Configuration File Inspection Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill shows a configuration scanner that includes .env-style files and reads configuration file contents. This is appropriate for validation, but it can place secrets into the agent context if users run it on real environments without redaction.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code example explicitly includes .env file patterns and reads each matching file. The behavior is legitimate for validation, but the sensitive-data exposure risk is direct.

medium_findings.title: Runtime File Watching Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill provides an example using chokidar to watch a configuration path persistently. This is not malicious, but it expands filesystem monitoring behavior and should be limited to intended configuration paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The watcher is clearly shown in a runtime validation example and uses a caller-provided path. The risk depends on how broadly the user applies the generated code.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick findings correspond to Markdown fenced code blocks, not executable shell backticks or runtime command execution. No command execution behavior was found in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows these lines are Markdown code fences or inline formatting. I did not find shell execution syntax or instructions to run arbitrary commands.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Identifier False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts do not match weak algorithms in the reviewed context. The actual encryption example uses AES-256-GCM with PBKDF2 and SHA-256, while the flagged lines are frontmatter, imports, test descriptions, or documentation formatting.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines do not contain DES, MD5, RC4, or other weak crypto usage. Nearby secure configuration code names aes-256-gcm and sha256 instead.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Validation Examples
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alerts point to host, localhost, schema validation, or file watcher terms inside configuration validation examples. No evidence found of host enumeration, network probing, or inventory collection beyond user-provided configuration data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding code validates supplied configuration fields and watches a supplied path. I did not find scanning of networks or system inventory commands.

low_findings.title: Environment Access Alerts Are Configuration Getter False Positives
low_findings.description: The env_access alerts map to dictionary get calls on configuration objects, not operating system environment variable reads. No process environment access was found at the flagged lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both findings are config.get calls inside examples. They do not access os.environ, process.env, or similar environment APIs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Reads Potentially Sensitive Config Files
dangerous_patterns.description: The example file discovery includes .env-style files and then reads file contents for secret checks. Generated implementations should redact or hash secret values before presenting results.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code example directly combines .env-style file matching with file content reads. The concern is data handling, not malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Configuration File Watcher
dangerous_patterns.description: The runtime validator example watches a provided configuration path with persistent monitoring. Users should scope this to approved config files and avoid broad project roots.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem watcher is explicit and persistent. It is a normal runtime validation pattern, but broad paths could observe more files than intended.

content.value_statement: Configuration errors can break deployments and expose secrets. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code design validation schemas, environment rules, tests, and security checks before release.
actual_capabilities: Analyzes application configuration structure and identifies validation needs.
Designs JSON Schema based validators with custom formats for URLs, ports, and durations.
Defines environment-specific rules for development, staging, and production settings.
Creates configuration test strategies for valid and invalid deployment values.
Outlines runtime validation, migration, encryption, and documentation patterns.
limitations: Does not execute validation automatically unless the user implements generated code.
May inspect sensitive configuration text if users provide real .env or secret files.
Requires project-specific schema details for accurate validation rules.
Generated examples still need review for each team’s security and compliance standards.

use_cases.title: Pre-release configuration checks
use_cases.description: Create validation rules that catch missing values, invalid ports, insecure URLs, and production debug flags before deployment.

use_cases.title: Secure configuration review
use_cases.description: Review service settings for likely secrets, weak defaults, missing encryption, and unsafe environment-specific behavior.

use_cases.title: Configuration documentation
use_cases.description: Turn schema fields and examples into a clear reference for required settings, defaults, types, and migration notes.

prompt_templates.title: Basic config review
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner audit of a single service configuration.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this deployment configuration for missing required values, unsafe defaults, and environment-specific problems. Do not reveal secret values in your answer.

prompt_templates.title: Create a validation schema
prompt_templates.scenario: Build structured validation rules from known settings.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a validation schema for these configuration fields. Include required fields, allowed ranges, secure URL rules, and clear error messages.

prompt_templates.title: Design release tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a configuration test plan for development, staging, and production. Include positive cases, negative cases, and security checks.

prompt_templates.title: Plan runtime validation and migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a runtime validation and migration approach for this service. Include watcher scope, rollback behavior, version migration rules, and secret redaction.

output_examples.input: A production service configuration allows debug mode and uses an insecure callback URL.
output_examples.output: The review flags debug mode as critical for production, marks the callback URL as high risk, and recommends HTTPS with environment-specific enforcement.

output_examples.input: A team needs validation rules for database host, port, database name, user, password, and SSL settings.
output_examples.output: The result describes required fields, valid port ranges, minimum password length, SSL requirements, and user-facing validation messages.

output_examples.input: A platform team wants documentation for configuration options and defaults.
output_examples.output: The generated reference lists each option, type, default behavior, example value, and migration note in a release-ready format.

best_practices: Use redacted sample values when asking for validation help.
Separate development, staging, and production rules before generating schemas.
Review generated validators with the same controls used for deployment code.
anti_patterns: Do not paste live secrets or full .env files into the agent context.
Do not apply runtime file watchers to broad project roots without scope limits.
Do not treat generated schemas as final without testing representative invalid configurations.

faq.question: What does this skill validate?
faq.answer: It helps validate deployment settings, schema rules, environment rules, runtime checks, migrations, and secure configuration handling.

faq.question: Can it generate JSON Schema rules?
faq.answer: Yes. It can draft schemas with required fields, custom formats, ranges, and validation error guidance.

faq.question: Does it run tests by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides test designs and code patterns that users must implement and run in their own project.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with secret files?
faq.answer: Use redacted examples. The skill discusses .env-style files, so live secrets should be removed before review.

faq.question: Can it support multiple environments?
faq.answer: Yes. It can define different rules for development, staging, and production configurations.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: DevOps, platform, backend, and security teams can use it before deployment or during configuration reviews.
